(**ROK**),[[c]](#cite_note-12) is a country in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). It constitutes the southern half of the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korea "Korea") and borders [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") along the [Korean Demilitarized Zone](/wiki/Korean_Demilitarized_Zone "Korean Demilitarized Zone"), with the [Yellow Sea](/wiki/Yellow_Sea "Yellow Sea") to the west and the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") to the east. South Korea claims to be the sole legitimate government of the entire peninsula and [adjacent islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_South_Korea "List of islands of South Korea"). It has [a population](/wiki/Demographics_of_South_Korea "Demographics of South Korea") of about 52 million, of which half live in the [Seoul metropolitan area](/wiki/Seoul_metropolitan_area "Seoul metropolitan area"), the [ninth most populous metropolitan area in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities"), with other major cities being [Busan](/wiki/Busan "Busan"), [Daegu](/wiki/Daegu "Daegu"), and [Incheon](/wiki/Incheon "Incheon").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Country in East Asia

"Republic of Korea" and "ROK" redirect here. For the Democratic People's Republic of Korea, see [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"). For the 1919–1945 government in exile that used the same name, see [Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_the_Republic_of_Korea "Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea"). **South Korea**,[[b]](#cite_note-11) officially the **Republic of Korea** (**ROK**),[[c]](#cite_note-12) is a country in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). It constitutes the southern half of the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korea "Korea") and borders [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") along the [Korean Demilitarized Zone](/wiki/Korean_Demilitarized_Zone "Korean Demilitarized Zone"), with the [Yellow Sea](/wiki/Yellow_Sea "Yellow Sea") to the west and the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") to the east. South Korea claims to be the sole legitimate government of the entire peninsula and [adjacent islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_South_Korea "List of islands of South Korea"). It has [a population](/wiki/Demographics_of_South_Korea "Demographics of South Korea") of about 52 million, of which half live in the [Seoul metropolitan area](/wiki/Seoul_metropolitan_area "Seoul metropolitan area"), the [ninth most populous metropolitan area in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities"), with other major cities being [Busan](/wiki/Busan "Busan"), [Daegu](/wiki/Daegu "Daegu"), and [Incheon](/wiki/Incheon "Incheon").

The Korean Peninsula was inhabited as early as the [Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic") period. [Its first kingdom](/wiki/Old_Chos%C5%8Fn "Old Chosŏn") was noted in Chinese records in the early seventh century BC. From the mid first century BC,[[10]](#cite_note-13) various [polities](/wiki/Polity "Polity") consolidated into the rival [kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms_of_Korea "Three Kingdoms of Korea") of [Goguryeo](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo"), [Baekje](/wiki/Baekje "Baekje"), and [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla"). The lattermost eventually [unified most of the peninsula](/wiki/Unified_Silla "Unified Silla") for the first time in the late seventh century AD, while [Balhae](/wiki/Balhae "Balhae") succeeded Goguryeo in the north. The [Goryeo](/wiki/Goryeo "Goryeo") dynasty (918–1392) achieved lasting unification and established the basis for the modern [Korean identity](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans"). The subsequent [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon") dynasty (1392–1897) generated cultural, economic, and scientific achievements and also established [isolationism](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") starting from the mid-17th century. The succeeding [Korean Empire](/wiki/Korean_Empire "Korean Empire") (1897–1910) sought [modernization and reform](/wiki/Gwangmu_Reform "Gwangmu Reform") but was [annexed in 1910](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93Korea_Treaty_of_1910 "Japan–Korea Treaty of 1910") into the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"). [Japanese rule](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule") ended following [Japan's surrender](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), after which Korea was [divided into two zones](/wiki/Division_of_Korea "Division of Korea"): the [Soviet-occupied northern zone](/wiki/Soviet_Civil_Administration "Soviet Civil Administration") and the [United States-occupied southern zone](/wiki/United_States_Army_Military_Government_in_Korea "United States Army Military Government in Korea").

After negotiations on [reunification](/wiki/Korean_reunification "Korean reunification") failed, the southern zone became the Republic of Korea in August 1948, while the northern zone became the [communist](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") [Democratic People's Republic of Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") the following month. In 1950, [a North Korean invasion](/wiki/Operation_Pokpung "Operation Pokpung") triggered the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), one of the first major [proxy conflicts](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), which saw extensive fighting involving the [American-led](/wiki/United_States_in_the_Korean_War "United States in the Korean War") [United Nations Command](/wiki/United_Nations_Command "United Nations Command") and the [Soviet-backed](/wiki/Soviet_Union_in_the_Korean_War "Soviet Union in the Korean War") [People's Volunteer Army](/wiki/People%27s_Volunteer_Army "People's Volunteer Army") from China. The war ended in 1953 with an [armistice](/wiki/Armistice "Armistice") and [left three million Koreans dead and the economy in ruins](/wiki/Aftermath_of_the_Korean_War "Aftermath of the Korean War"); due to the lack of a [peace treaty](/wiki/Peace_treaty "Peace treaty"), the [Korean conflict](/wiki/Korean_conflict "Korean conflict") is still ongoing. South Korea endured a series of dictatorships punctuated by coups, revolutions, and violent uprisings, but also experienced a [soaring economy](/wiki/Miracle_on_the_Han_River "Miracle on the Han River") and [one of the fastest rises in average GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(real)_per_capita_growth_rate "List of countries by GDP (real) per capita growth rate"), leading to its emergence as one of the [Four Asian Tigers](/wiki/Four_Asian_Tigers "Four Asian Tigers"). The [June Democratic Struggle](/wiki/June_Democratic_Struggle "June Democratic Struggle") of 1987 [ended authoritarian rule](/wiki/1987_South_Korean_presidential_election "1987 South Korean presidential election") and led to the establishment of the current [Sixth Republic](/wiki/Sixth_Republic_of_South_Korea "Sixth Republic of South Korea").

South Korea is now considered among the [most advanced democracies](/wiki/Democracy_indices "Democracy indices") in Asia. Under the [1987 constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_South_Korea "Constitution of South Korea"), it maintains a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [presidential republic](/wiki/Presidential_republic "Presidential republic") with a popularly elected unicameral legislature, the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(South_Korea) "National Assembly (South Korea)"). South Korea is a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the United States and is regarded as a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") in East Asia and an [emerging power](/wiki/Emerging_power "Emerging power") in global affairs; its [conscription](/wiki/Conscription_in_South_Korea "Conscription in South Korea")-based [armed forces](/wiki/Republic_of_Korea_Armed_Forces "Republic of Korea Armed Forces") are ranked as one of the strongest in the world and have the second highest number of [military and paramilitary personnel](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel"). A highly [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), [South Korea's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_South_Korea "Economy of South Korea") is ranked 14th largest in the world [by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") and [PPP-adjusted GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)"); it is the world's [eleventh-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") and [seventh-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports"). The country performs well in metrics of [education](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment#PISA_2022_ranking_summary "Programme for International Student Assessment"), [human development](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), [democratic governance](/wiki/The_Economist_Democracy_Index "The Economist Democracy Index"), and [innovation](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index"). It has [one of the world's longest life expectances](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy"), though its population is [aging rapidly](/wiki/Aging_of_South_Korea "Aging of South Korea") and has the lowest fertility rate in the world. South Korea has some of the [fastest Internet connection speeds](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_Internet_connection_speeds "List of sovereign states by Internet connection speeds") and [densest high-speed railway networks](/wiki/List_of_high-speed_railway_lines "List of high-speed railway lines"). Since the turn of the 21st century, the country has been renowned for its globally influential pop culture, particularly [in music](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop"), [TV dramas](/wiki/Korean_drama "Korean drama"), and [cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_South_Korea "Cinema of South Korea"), a phenomenon referred to as the [Korean Wave](/wiki/Korean_Wave "Korean Wave"). South Korea is a member of the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD")'s [Development Assistance Committee](/wiki/Development_Assistance_Committee "Development Assistance Committee"), the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"), the [IPEF](/wiki/Indo-Pacific_Economic_Framework "Indo-Pacific Economic Framework"), and the [Paris Club](/wiki/Paris_Club "Paris Club").

## Etymology

See also: [Names of Korea](/wiki/Names_of_Korea "Names of Korea")

The name *Korea* is an [exonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") derived from the historical Korean kingdom name *[Goryeo](/wiki/Goryeo "Goryeo")* ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): 고려; [Hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja"): 高麗; [MR](/wiki/McCune%E2%80%93Reischauer "McCune–Reischauer"): *Koryŏ*). *Goryeo* was the shortened name officially adopted by [Goguryeo](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo") in the 5th century[[11]](#cite_note-14)[[12]](#cite_note-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16) and the name of its 10th-century successor state Goryeo.[[14]](#cite_note-Koryo1-17)[[15]](#cite_note-Koryo2-18) Visiting Arab and Persian merchants pronounced its name as "Korea".[[16]](#cite_note-19) The modern name of Korea appears in the first Portuguese [maps](/wiki/Portolan_chart "Portolan chart") of 1568 by João vaz Dourado as *Conrai*[[17]](#cite_note-20) and later in the late 16th century and early 17th century as *Corea* (Korea) in the maps of Teixeira Albernaz of 1630.[[18]](#cite_note-21)

The Kingdom of Goryeo became first known to Westerners when [Afonso de Albuquerque](/wiki/Afonso_de_Albuquerque "Afonso de Albuquerque") [conquered Malacca](/wiki/Capture_of_Malacca_(1511) "Capture of Malacca (1511)") in 1511 and described the people who traded in this part of the world as the *Gores*.[[19]](#cite_note-22) Despite the coexistence of the spellings *Corea* and *Korea* in 19th-century publications, some Koreans believe that [Imperial Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") intentionally standardized the spelling of *Korea* in order to make Japan appear first alphabetically during occupation.[[20]](#cite_note-UDN-23)[[21]](#cite_note-LATimes0309-24)

After Goryeo was replaced by the Kingdom of [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon") (조선; 朝鮮; *Chosŏn*) in 1392, Joseon became the official name for the entire territory, though it was not universally accepted. The new official name was derived from the ancient kingdom of [Gojoseon](/wiki/Old_Chos%C5%8Fn "Old Chosŏn"). In 1897, [King Gojong](/wiki/Gojong_of_Korea "Gojong of Korea") changed the country's official name from *Joseon* to the [Korean Empire](/wiki/Korean_Empire "Korean Empire") (대한제국; 大韓帝國; *Daehan Jeguk*; *Taehan Cheguk*; lit. 'Great Han Empire'). The latter half of the Korean Empire's name *Daehan* (대한; 大韓; lit. 'Great Han') derives from [Samhan](/wiki/Samhan "Samhan") (Three Han), referring to the [Three Kingdoms of Korea](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms_of_Korea "Three Kingdoms of Korea"), not the ancient confederacies in the southern Korean Peninsula.[[22]](#cite_note-25)[[23]](#cite_note-26) However, the name *Joseon* was still widely used by Koreans to refer to their country, even though it was no longer the official name. Under [Japanese rule](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule"), the two names *Han* and *Joseon* coexisted.

Following the [surrender of Japan](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan"), in 1945, the "Republic of Korea" was adopted as the legal English name for the new country.[[24]](#cite_note-unloved-27) The Korean name *Daehan Minguk* (대한민국; 大韓民國; lit. 'Great Han Republic') is sometimes used by South Koreans as a [metonym](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy") to refer to the Korean nation as a whole, rather than just the South Korean state.[[25]](#cite_note-thefreelibrary.com-28)[[24]](#cite_note-unloved-27)

## History

Main articles: [History of Korea](/wiki/History_of_Korea "History of Korea") and [History of South Korea](/wiki/History_of_South_Korea "History of South Korea")

### Ancient Korea

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Front_view_of_Seokguram_from_front_chamber.jpg/250px-Front_view_of_Seokguram_from_front_chamber.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_view_of_Seokguram_from_front_chamber.jpg)

[Seokguram Grotto](/wiki/Seokguram_Grotto "Seokguram Grotto") from the [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla") era, a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")

The Korean Peninsula was inhabited as early as the [Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic") period.[[26]](#cite_note-29)[[27]](#cite_note-30) According to Korea's [founding mythology](/wiki/Civil_religion "Civil religion"), the history of Korea begins with the founding of Joseon (also known as "[Gojoseon](/wiki/Old_Chos%C5%8Fn "Old Chosŏn")", or "Old Joseon", to differentiate it from the 14th century dynasty) in 2333 BC by the legendary [Dangun](/wiki/Dangun "Dangun").[[28]](#cite_note-koreashistory-31)[[29]](#cite_note-DangunGija-32) Gojoseon was noted in Chinese records in the early 7th century.[[30]](#cite_note-33) Gojoseon expanded until it controlled the northern Korean Peninsula and parts of [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"). [Gija Joseon](/wiki/Gija_Joseon "Gija Joseon") was purportedly founded in the 12th century BC, but its existence and role have been controversial in the modern era.[[29]](#cite_note-DangunGija-32)[[31]](#cite_note-34) In 108 BC, the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") [defeated](/wiki/Gojoseon%E2%80%93Han_War "Gojoseon–Han War") [Wiman Joseon](/wiki/Wiman_Joseon "Wiman Joseon") and installed [four commanderies](/wiki/Four_Commanderies_of_Han "Four Commanderies of Han") in the northern Korean peninsula. Three of the commanderies fell or retreated westward within a few decades, but the [Lelang commandery](/wiki/Lelang_commandery "Lelang commandery") remained a center of cultural and economic exchange with successive Chinese dynasties ruling for four centuries,[[32]](#cite_note-35) until it was conquered by Goguryeo in 313.[[33]](#cite_note-Gojoseon-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)

The linguistic homeland of Proto-[Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") is located somewhere in southern [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia")/[Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), such as the [Liao River](/wiki/Liao_River "Liao River") area or the [Amur](/wiki/Amur "Amur") River area. Proto-Koreans arrived in the southern part of the Korean Peninsula at around 300 BC, replacing and assimilating [Japonic](/wiki/Japonic_languages "Japonic languages")-speaking [Yayoi](/wiki/Yayoi_people "Yayoi people") and likely causing their migration to the [Japanese archipelago](/wiki/Japanese_archipelago "Japanese archipelago").[[36]](#cite_note-39)[[37]](#cite_note-40)

### Three Kingdoms of Korea

During the [Proto–Three Kingdoms period](/wiki/Proto%E2%80%93Three_Kingdoms_period "Proto–Three Kingdoms period"), the states of [Buyeo](/wiki/Buyeo "Buyeo"), [Okjeo](/wiki/Okjeo "Okjeo"), [Dongye](/wiki/Eastern_Ye "Eastern Ye"), and [Samhan](/wiki/Samhan "Samhan") occupied the whole Korean peninsula and southern Manchuria. From them, the [Three Kingdoms of Korea](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms_of_Korea "Three Kingdoms of Korea") emerged: [Goguryeo](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo"), [Baekje](/wiki/Baekje "Baekje"), and [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla").

Goguryeo, the largest and most powerful among them, was a highly militaristic state[[38]](#cite_note-41) and competed with various Chinese dynasties during its 700 years of history. Goguryeo experienced a golden age under [Gwanggaeto the Great](/wiki/Gwanggaeto_the_Great "Gwanggaeto the Great") and his son [Jangsu](/wiki/Jangsu_of_Goguryeo "Jangsu of Goguryeo"),[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45) who both subdued Baekje and Silla during their respective reigns, achieving a brief unification of the Three Kingdoms and becoming the most dominant power on the Korean Peninsula.[[43]](#cite_note-Jinwung-46)[[44]](#cite_note-47) In addition to contesting control of the Korean Peninsula, Goguryeo had many [military conflicts](/wiki/Military_history_of_Goguryeo "Military history of Goguryeo") with various [Chinese dynasties](/wiki/Dynasties_of_China "Dynasties of China"), most notably the [Goguryeo–Sui War](/wiki/Goguryeo%E2%80%93Sui_War "Goguryeo–Sui War"), in which Goguryeo defeated a huge force said to number over a million men.[[45]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Korean_book-Jikji-Selected_Teachings_of_Buddhist_Sages_and_Seon_Masters-1377.jpg/250px-Korean_book-Jikji-Selected_Teachings_of_Buddhist_Sages_and_Seon_Masters-1377.jpg)](/wiki/File:Korean_book-Jikji-Selected_Teachings_of_Buddhist_Sages_and_Seon_Masters-1377.jpg)

The oldest surviving metal movable type book, the *[Jikji](/wiki/Jikji "Jikji")*, was printed in 1377, and [Goryeo](/wiki/Goryeo "Goryeo") created the world's first metal-based movable type in 1234.[[46]](#cite_note-print_c-49)

Baekje was a maritime power,[[47]](#cite_note-50) sometimes called the "[Phoenicia](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") of East Asia".[[48]](#cite_note-51) Its maritime ability was instrumental in the dissemination of [Buddhism throughout East Asia](/wiki/East_Asian_Buddhism "East Asian Buddhism") and spreading continental culture to Japan.[[49]](#cite_note-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53) Baekje was once a great military power on the Korean Peninsula, especially during the time of [Geunchogo](/wiki/Geunchogo_of_Baekje "Geunchogo of Baekje"),[[51]](#cite_note-54) but was critically defeated by Gwanggaeto the Great and declined.[[52]](#cite_note-55) Silla was the smallest and weakest of the three, but used opportunistic pacts and alliances with the more powerful Korean kingdoms, and eventually [Tang China](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), to its advantage.[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57)

In 676, the unification of the Three Kingdoms by Silla led to the [Northern and Southern States period](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_States_period "Northern and Southern States period"), in which relationships between Korea and China remained relatively peaceful. [Balhae](/wiki/Balhae "Balhae"), a Goguryeo successor state founded by a [general](/wiki/Go_of_Balhae "Go of Balhae"), controlled most of Manchuria and parts of the Russian Far East and was called the "Prosperous Country in the East".[[55]](#cite_note-58) In addition to Koreans, there were many other ethnicities such as the [Mohe](/wiki/Mohe_people "Mohe people"), [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples"), and Chinese.

[Late Silla](/wiki/Late_Silla "Late Silla") was a wealthy country,[[56]](#cite_note-59) and its metropolitan capital of [Gyeongju](/wiki/Gyeongju "Gyeongju")[[57]](#cite_note-60) grew to become the fourth largest city in the world.[[58]](#cite_note-61)[[59]](#cite_note-62)[[60]](#cite_note-63)[[61]](#cite_note-64) It experienced a golden age of art and culture,[[62]](#cite_note-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-67)[[65]](#cite_note-68) exemplified by monuments such as [Hwangnyongsa](/wiki/Hwangnyongsa "Hwangnyongsa"), [Seokguram](/wiki/Seokguram "Seokguram"), and the [Emille Bell](/wiki/Bell_of_King_Seongdeok "Bell of King Seongdeok"). It also carried on the maritime legacy and prowess of Baekje, and during the 8th and 9th centuries dominated the seas of East Asia and the trade between China, Korea, and Japan, most notably during the time of [Chang Pogo](/wiki/Chang_Pogo "Chang Pogo"). In addition, Silla people made overseas communities in China on the [Shandong Peninsula](/wiki/Shandong_Peninsula "Shandong Peninsula") and the mouth of the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") River.[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72) However, Silla was later weakened due to internal strife and the revival of successor states [Baekje](/wiki/Later_Baekje "Later Baekje") and [Goguryeo](/wiki/Taebong "Taebong"), which culminated into the [Later Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Later_Three_Kingdoms "Later Three Kingdoms") period in the late 9th century.

Buddhism flourished during this time. Many [Korean Buddhists](/wiki/Korean_Buddhism "Korean Buddhism") gained great fame among Chinese Buddhist circles[[70]](#cite_note-73) and greatly contributed to [Chinese Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism").[[71]](#cite_note-74) Examples of significant Korean Buddhists from this period include [Woncheuk](/wiki/Woncheuk "Woncheuk"), [Wonhyo](/wiki/Wonhyo "Wonhyo"), [Uisang](/wiki/Uisang "Uisang"), [Musang](/wiki/Kim_Hwasang "Kim Hwasang"),[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77)[[75]](#cite_note-78) and [Kim Gyo-gak](/wiki/Kim_Gyo-gak "Kim Gyo-gak"). Kim was a Silla prince whose influence made [Mount Jiuhua](/wiki/Mount_Jiuhua "Mount Jiuhua") one of the Four [Sacred Mountains](/wiki/Sacred_Mountains_of_China "Sacred Mountains of China") of Chinese Buddhism.[[76]](#cite_note-79)

### Unified dynasties

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/%EC%B0%BD%EB%8D%95%EA%B6%81_%EC%A0%84%EA%B2%BD_%282012%29.jpg/250px-%EC%B0%BD%EB%8D%95%EA%B6%81_%EC%A0%84%EA%B2%BD_%282012%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%EC%B0%BD%EB%8D%95%EA%B6%81_%EC%A0%84%EA%B2%BD_(2012).jpg)

[Changdeokgung](/wiki/Changdeokgung "Changdeokgung"), pictured in 2014, one of the Five Grand Palaces of Seoul built during the Joseon dynasty and a [UNESCO World Heritage](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage "UNESCO World Heritage") Site

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/%EC%95%84%EB%A6%84%EB%8B%A4%EC%9A%B4_%EB%B0%A9%ED%99%94%EC%88%98%EB%A5%98%EC%A0%95.jpg/250px-%EC%95%84%EB%A6%84%EB%8B%A4%EC%9A%B4_%EB%B0%A9%ED%99%94%EC%88%98%EB%A5%98%EC%A0%95.jpg)](/wiki/File:%EC%95%84%EB%A6%84%EB%8B%A4%EC%9A%B4_%EB%B0%A9%ED%99%94%EC%88%98%EB%A5%98%EC%A0%95.jpg)

Banghwa Suryu Pavilion, part of [Hwaseong Fortress](/wiki/Hwaseong_Fortress "Hwaseong Fortress"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") (2019)

In 936, the Later Three Kingdoms were united by [Wang Geon](/wiki/Taejo_of_Goryeo "Taejo of Goryeo"), who established [Goryeo](/wiki/Goryeo "Goryeo") as a successor state to Goguryeo.[[14]](#cite_note-Koryo1-17)[[15]](#cite_note-Koryo2-18)[[77]](#cite_note-Koryo3-80)[[78]](#cite_note-Koryo4-81) Balhae had fallen to the [Khitan Empire](/wiki/Khitan_Empire "Khitan Empire") in 926, and a decade later the [last crown prince of Balhae](/wiki/Dae_Gwang-hyeon "Dae Gwang-hyeon") fled south to Goryeo, where he was warmly welcomed and included in the ruling family by Wang Geon, thus unifying the two successor nations of Goguryeo.[[79]](#cite_note-82) Like Silla, Goryeo was a highly cultural state, and invented the metal movable type [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press").[[46]](#cite_note-print_c-49) After defeating the Khitan Empire, which was the most powerful empire of its time[[80]](#cite_note-83)[[81]](#cite_note-84) in the [Goryeo–Khitan War](/wiki/Goryeo%E2%80%93Khitan_War "Goryeo–Khitan War"), Goryeo experienced a golden age that lasted a century, during which the [Tripitaka Koreana](/wiki/Tripitaka_Koreana "Tripitaka Koreana") was completed and significant developments in printing and publishing occurred. This promoted education and the dispersion of knowledge on philosophy, literature, religion, and science. By 1100, there were 12 universities that produced notable scholars.[[82]](#cite_note-85)[[83]](#cite_note-86)

However, the [Mongol invasions](/wiki/Mongol_invasions_of_Korea "Mongol invasions of Korea") in the 13th century greatly weakened the kingdom. Goryeo was never conquered by the Mongols, but exhausted after three decades of fighting, the Korean court sent its [crown prince](/wiki/Wonjong_of_Goryeo "Wonjong of Goryeo") to the [Yuan](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") capital to swear allegiance to [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan"), who accepted and married one of his daughters to the Korean crown prince.[[84]](#cite_note-Mongols-87) Henceforth, Goryeo continued to rule Korea, though as a tributary ally to the Mongols for the next 86 years. During this period, the two nations' royalty became intertwined as all subsequent Korean kings married Mongol princesses.[[84]](#cite_note-Mongols-87) In the mid-14th century, Goryeo drove out the Mongols to regain its northern territories, briefly conquered [Liaoyang](/wiki/Liaoyang "Liaoyang"), and defeated invasions by the [Red Turbans](/wiki/Red_Turbans_invasions_of_Goryeo "Red Turbans invasions of Goryeo"). However, in 1392, General [Yi Seong-gye](/wiki/Taejo_of_Joseon "Taejo of Joseon"), who had been ordered to attack China, turned his army around and staged a successful coup.

Yi Seong-gye established the [House of Yi](/wiki/House_of_Yi "House of Yi"), renamed the nation to [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon") in reference to Gojoseon, and moved the capital to Hanseong (one of the old names of [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul")).[[85]](#cite_note-88) The first 200 years of the Joseon dynasty were marked by peace and saw great advancements in science[[86]](#cite_note-89)[[87]](#cite_note-90) and education,[[88]](#cite_note-91) among them the creation of [Hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul") by [Sejong the Great](/wiki/Sejong_the_Great "Sejong the Great") to promote literacy among the common people.[[89]](#cite_note-Hangul-92) The prevailing ideology of the time was [Neo-Confucianism](/wiki/Korean_Confucianism "Korean Confucianism"), which was epitomized by the [seonbi](/wiki/Seonbi "Seonbi") class: nobles who passed up positions of wealth and power to lead lives of study and integrity. Between 1592 and 1598, Japan under [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") launched [invasions of Korea](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%931598) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)"), but the advance was halted by Korean forces (most notably the [Joseon Navy](/wiki/Joseon_Navy "Joseon Navy") led by Admiral [Yi Sun-sin](/wiki/Yi_Sun-sin "Yi Sun-sin") and his renowned "[turtle ship](/wiki/Turtle_ship "Turtle ship")") with assistance from [righteous army](/wiki/Righteous_army "Righteous army") militias formed by Korean civilians, and [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") Chinese troops.[[90]](#cite_note-Perez_2013_140–141-93) Through a series of successful battles of attrition, the Japanese forces were eventually forced to withdraw, and relations between all parties became normalized. However, the [Manchus](/wiki/Manchu_people "Manchu people") took advantage of Joseon's war-weakened state and [invaded in 1627 and 1637](/wiki/Manchu_invasion_of_Korea "Manchu invasion of Korea"). After normalizing relations with the new [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), Joseon experienced a nearly 200-year period of peace. Kings [Yeongjo](/wiki/Yeongjo_of_Joseon "Yeongjo of Joseon") and [Jeongjo](/wiki/Jeongjo_of_Joseon "Jeongjo of Joseon") particularly led a new renaissance of the Joseon dynasty during the 18th century.[[91]](#cite_note-94)[[92]](#cite_note-95)

In the 19th century, Joseon began experiencing economic difficulties and widespread uprisings, including the [Donghak Peasant Revolution](/wiki/Donghak_Peasant_Revolution "Donghak Peasant Revolution"). The royal in-law families had gained control of the government, leading to mass corruption and weakening of the state.[[93]](#cite_note-96) Additionally, the strict isolationism of the Joseon government that earned it the nickname "the [hermit kingdom](/wiki/Hermit_kingdom "Hermit kingdom")" became increasingly ineffective due to increasing encroachment from powers such as Japan, Russia, and the United States. This was exemplified by the [Joseon–United States Treaty of 1882](/wiki/Joseon%E2%80%93United_States_Treaty_of_1882 "Joseon–United States Treaty of 1882"), which forced Joseon to open its borders.

### Japanese occupation and World War II

Main article: [Korea under Japanese rule](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/L%E2%80%99Ind%C3%A9pendance_de_la_Cor%C3%A9e_et_la_Paix-02.jpg/250px-L%E2%80%99Ind%C3%A9pendance_de_la_Cor%C3%A9e_et_la_Paix-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%E2%80%99Ind%C3%A9pendance_de_la_Cor%C3%A9e_et_la_Paix-02.jpg)

[March First Movement](/wiki/March_First_Movement "March First Movement") [1919](/wiki/1919 "1919"), protests in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul") against [Japanese colonial rule](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule")

In the late 19th century, Japan became a significant regional power after winning the [First Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") against Qing China and the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") against the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"). In 1897, King Gojong, the [last king of Korea](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Korea "List of monarchs of Korea"), proclaimed Joseon as the [Korean Empire](/wiki/Korean_Empire "Korean Empire"). However, Japan [compelled Korea to become its protectorate](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93Korea_Treaty_of_1905 "Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905") in 1905 and [formally annexed it](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93Korea_Treaty_of_1910 "Japan–Korea Treaty of 1910") in 1910. What followed was a period of forced assimilation, in which Korean language, culture, and history were suppressed.[[94]](#cite_note-97) This led to the [March First Movement](/wiki/March_First_Movement "March First Movement") protests in 1919 and the subsequent foundation of resistance groups in exile, primarily in China. Among the resistance groups was [Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_the_Republic_of_Korea "Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea").[[95]](#cite_note-98)

Towards the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the U.S. proposed dividing the Korean peninsula into two occupation zones: a [U.S. zone](/wiki/United_States_Army_Military_Government_in_Korea "United States Army Military Government in Korea") and a [Soviet zone](/wiki/Soviet_Civil_Administration "Soviet Civil Administration"). [Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk") and [Charles H. Bonesteel III](/wiki/Charles_H._Bonesteel_III "Charles H. Bonesteel III") suggested the [38th parallel](/wiki/38th_parallel_north "38th parallel north") as the dividing line, as it placed Seoul under U.S. control. To the surprise of Rusk and Bonesteel, the Soviets accepted their proposal and agreed to divide Korea.[[96]](#cite_note-99)

### Division of Korea

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Ceremony_inaugurating_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_Korea_%28colored%29.jpg/250px-Ceremony_inaugurating_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_Korea_%28colored%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceremony_inaugurating_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_Korea_(colored).jpg)

Ceremony for the inauguration of the South Korean government (August 15, 1948)

Despite intentions to liberate a unified peninsula in the [1943 Cairo Declaration](/wiki/1943_Cairo_Declaration "1943 Cairo Declaration"), escalating tensions between the Soviet Union and the United States led to the [division of Korea](/wiki/Division_of_Korea "Division of Korea") into two political entities in 1948: North Korea and South Korea.

In the South, the United States appointed and supported the former head of the Korean Provisional Government [Syngman Rhee](/wiki/Syngman_Rhee "Syngman Rhee") as leader. Rhee won the first presidential elections of the newly declared Republic of Korea in May 1948. In the North, the Soviets backed a former anti-Japanese guerrilla and communist activist, [Kim Il Sung](/wiki/Kim_Il_Sung "Kim Il Sung"), who was appointed premier of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea in September.[[97]](#cite_note-100)

In October, the Soviet Union declared Kim Il Sung's government as sovereign over both the north and south. The UN declared Rhee's government as "a lawful government having effective control and jurisdiction over that part of Korea where the UN Temporary Commission on Korea was able to observe and consult" and the government "based on elections which was observed by the Temporary Commission" in addition to a statement that "this is the only such government in Korea."[[98]](#cite_note-UN195-101) Both leaders engaged in authoritarian repression of political opponents.[[99]](#cite_note-102) South Korea requested military support from the United States but was denied,[[100]](#cite_note-103) while North Korea's military was heavily reinforced by the Soviet Union.[[101]](#cite_note-104)[[102]](#cite_note-105)

### Korean War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/WarMemorialOfKorea.jpg/250px-WarMemorialOfKorea.jpg)](/wiki/File:WarMemorialOfKorea.jpg)

[War Memorial of Korea](/wiki/War_Memorial_of_Korea "War Memorial of Korea").

On June 25, 1950, [North Korea invaded](/wiki/Operation_Pokpung "Operation Pokpung") South Korea, sparking the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War")'s first major conflict, which continued until 1953. At the time, the Soviet Union had boycotted the UN, thus forfeiting [their veto rights](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_veto_power "United Nations Security Council veto power"). This allowed the UN to intervene in a civil war when it became apparent that the superior North Korean forces would unify the entire country. The Soviet Union and China backed North Korea, with the later participation of millions of [Chinese troops](/wiki/People%27s_Volunteer_Army "People's Volunteer Army"). After an ebb and flow that saw both sides facing defeat with massive losses among Korean civilians in both the north and the south, the war eventually reached a stalemate. During the war, Rhee's party promoted the [One-People Principle](/wiki/Ilminism "Ilminism"), an effort to build an obedient citizenry through ethnic homogeneity and authoritarian appeals to [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism").[[103]](#cite_note-Hwang93-106)

The [1953 armistice](/wiki/Korean_Armistice_Agreement "Korean Armistice Agreement"), never signed by South Korea, split the peninsula along the [demilitarized zone](/wiki/Korean_Demilitarized_Zone "Korean Demilitarized Zone") near the original demarcation line. No peace treaty was ever signed, resulting in the two countries remaining technically at war. Approximately 3 million people died in the Korean War, with a higher proportional civilian death toll than [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") or the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), making it one of the deadliest conflicts of the Cold War era.[[104]](#cite_note-Kim_p.45-107)[[105]](#cite_note-Cumings_p._35-108) In addition, virtually all of Korea's major cities were destroyed by the war.[[106]](#cite_note-Lewy_pp._450-453-109)

### Miracle on the Han River

Main article: [Miracle on the Han River](/wiki/Miracle_on_the_Han_River "Miracle on the Han River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/South_Korea%27s_GDP_%28PPP%29_growth_from_1911_to_2008.png/250px-South_Korea%27s_GDP_%28PPP%29_growth_from_1911_to_2008.png)](/wiki/File:South_Korea%27s_GDP_(PPP)_growth_from_1911_to_2008.png)

Between 1962 and 1994, the [South Korean economy](/wiki/Economy_of_South_Korea "Economy of South Korea") grew at an average of 10% annually, fueled by annual export growth of 20%,[[107]](#cite_note-worldbank.org-110) in a period called the [Miracle on the Han River](/wiki/Miracle_on_the_Han_River "Miracle on the Han River").

In 1960, a student uprising (the "[April Revolution](/wiki/April_Revolution "April Revolution")") led to the resignation of the autocratic President Syngman Rhee. This was followed by 13 months of political instability as South Korea was led by the weak and ineffectual [Second Republic](/wiki/Second_Republic_of_Korea "Second Republic of Korea"). This instability was broken by the [May 16, 1961 coup](/wiki/May_16_coup "May 16 coup") led by General [Park Chung Hee](/wiki/Park_Chung_Hee "Park Chung Hee"). As president, Park oversaw a period of rapid [export-led economic growth](/wiki/Export-oriented_industrialization "Export-oriented industrialization") enforced by [political repression](/wiki/Political_repression_in_South_Korea "Political repression in South Korea"). Under Park, South Korea [took an active role](/wiki/South_Korea_in_the_Vietnam_War "South Korea in the Vietnam War") in the Vietnam War.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

Park was heavily criticized as a ruthless military dictator, who in 1972 extended his rule by creating a [new constitution](/wiki/Yusin_Constitution "Yusin Constitution"), which gave the president dictatorial powers and permitted him to run for an unlimited number of six-year terms. The [Korean economy](/wiki/Economy_of_South_Korea "Economy of South Korea") developed significantly during Park's tenure, largely due to investment in [family-run conglomerates](/wiki/Chaebol "Chaebol"). The government developed the [nationwide expressway system](/wiki/Expressways_in_South_Korea "Expressways in South Korea"), the [Seoul subway system](/wiki/Seoul_Metropolitan_Subway "Seoul Metropolitan Subway"), and laid the foundation for economic development during his 17-year tenure, which ended with [his assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_Park_Chung_Hee "Assassination of Park Chung Hee") on 26 October, 1979.

The years after Park's assassination were marked again by political turmoil, as the previously suppressed opposition leaders all campaigned to run for president in the sudden political void. In 1979, General [Chun Doo-hwan](/wiki/Chun_Doo-hwan "Chun Doo-hwan") led the [coup d'état of December Twelfth](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_of_December_Twelfth "Coup d'état of December Twelfth"). On May 17, 1980, Chun forced the Cabinet to expand martial law to the whole nation, which had previously not applied to [Jeju Island](/wiki/Jeju_Island "Jeju Island"). The expanded martial law closed universities, banned political activities, and further curtailed the press. Chun's assumption of the presidency through the events of May 17 triggered nationwide protests demanding democracy; these protests were particularly widespread in [Gwangju](/wiki/Gwangju "Gwangju"), to which Chun sent special forces to violently suppress the 18–27 May 1980 [Gwangju Democratization Movement](/wiki/Gwangju_Uprising "Gwangju Uprising")[[109]](#cite_note-112) at the cost of probably 500 to 2,000 civilian lives[[110]](#cite_note-113) and 26 dead soldiers (half of them by [friendly fire](/wiki/Friendly_fire "Friendly fire")).

Chun subsequently created the National Defense Emergency Policy Committee and took the presidency according to his political plan. Chun and his government held South Korea under a despotic rule until 1987, when a [Seoul National University](/wiki/Seoul_National_University "Seoul National University") student, [Park Jong-chul](/wiki/Park_Jong-chul "Park Jong-chul"), was tortured to death.[[111]](#cite_note-114) On June 10, the [Catholic Priests Association for Justice](/wiki/Catholic_Priests_Association_for_Justice "Catholic Priests Association for Justice") revealed the incident, igniting the [June Democratic Struggle](/wiki/June_Democratic_Struggle "June Democratic Struggle") across the country. Eventually, Chun's party, the [Democratic Justice Party](/wiki/Democratic_Justice_Party "Democratic Justice Party"), and its leader, [Roh Tae-woo](/wiki/Roh_Tae-woo "Roh Tae-woo"), announced the [June 29 Declaration](/wiki/June_29_Declaration "June 29 Declaration"), which promised a democratic election of the president.

### Democracy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Kim_Dae-jung_%282001%29.tif/lossless-page1-250px-Kim_Dae-jung_%282001%29.tif.png)](/wiki/File:Kim_Dae-jung_(2001).tif)

President [Kim Dae-jung](/wiki/Kim_Dae-jung "Kim Dae-jung"), the 2000 [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") recipient for advancing democracy and human rights in South Korea and East Asia and for reconciliation with North Korea, was sometimes called the "[Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela") of Asia".[[112]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-2009-115)

Roh Tae-woo went on to win the [1987 election](/wiki/1987_South_Korean_presidential_election "1987 South Korean presidential election") by a narrow margin against the two main opposition leaders, [Kim Dae-jung](/wiki/Kim_Dae-jung "Kim Dae-jung") and [Kim Young-sam](/wiki/Kim_Young-sam "Kim Young-sam"). Seoul hosted the [Olympic Games in 1988](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics"), which was widely regarded as successful and a significant boost for South Korea's global image and economy.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

[South Korea was formally invited](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_702 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 702") to become a member of the United Nations in 1991. The transition of Korea from autocracy to modern democracy was marked in 1997 by the election of Kim Dae-jung, who was sworn in as the eighth president of South Korea on February 25, 1998. His election was significant given that he had in earlier years been a political prisoner sentenced to death (later commuted to exile). He won against the backdrop of the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"), where he took [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF") advice to restructure the economy and the nation soon recovered its economic growth, albeit at a slower pace.[[112]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-2009-115)

In June 2000, as part of President Kim Dae-jung's "[Sunshine Policy](/wiki/Sunshine_Policy "Sunshine Policy")" of engagement, a [North–South summit](/wiki/Inter-Korean_summit "Inter-Korean summit") took place in [Pyongyang](/wiki/Pyongyang "Pyongyang"), the capital of North Korea, now ruled by Kim Il Sung's son [Kim Jong Il](/wiki/Kim_Jong_Il "Kim Jong Il").[[114]](#cite_note-117) Later that year, Kim received the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") "for his work for democracy and human rights in South Korea and in East Asia in general, and for peace and reconciliation with North Korea in particular".[[115]](#cite_note-118) However, because of discontent among the population for fruitless approaches to the North under the previous administrations and, amid North Korean provocations, a conservative government was elected in 2007 led by President [Lee Myung-bak](/wiki/Lee_Myung-bak "Lee Myung-bak"), former [mayor of Seoul](/wiki/Mayor_of_Seoul "Mayor of Seoul").[[116]](#cite_note-119) While [South Korean and Japanese relations](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93Korea_relations "Japan–Korea relations") improved when they jointly co-hosted the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), it later [soured](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93Korea_disputes "Japan–Korea disputes") because of conflicting claims of sovereignty over the [Liancourt Rocks](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks_dispute "Liancourt Rocks dispute").[[117]](#cite_note-120)[[118]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/2010_G-20_Seoul_summit.jpg/250px-2010_G-20_Seoul_summit.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010_G-20_Seoul_summit.jpg)

South Korea became the first non-[G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") chair of the [G-20](/wiki/G-20 "G-20") when it hosted the [2010 Seoul summit](/wiki/2010_G-20_Seoul_summit "2010 G-20 Seoul summit").[[119]](#cite_note-122)

In 2010, there was an [escalation in attacks](/wiki/List_of_border_incidents_involving_North_and_South_Korea "List of border incidents involving North and South Korea") by North Korea. In March 2010 the South Korean warship [ROKS *Cheonan* was sunk](/wiki/ROKS_Cheonan_sinking "ROKS Cheonan sinking") killing 46 South Korean sailors, allegedly by a North Korean submarine. In November 2010 [Yeonpyeongdo](/wiki/Yeonpyeongdo "Yeonpyeongdo") [was attacked](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Yeonpyeong "Bombardment of Yeonpyeong") by a significant North Korean artillery barrage, with 4 people dying. The official UN report declined to explicitly name North Korea as the perpetrator for the *Cheonan* sinking. The lack of a strong response to these attacks from both South Korea and the international community caused significant anger with the South Korean public.[[120]](#cite_note-123)

### Contemporary history

The children of presidents Park Chung Hee and Kim Jong Il would take power in the two Koreas from 2011 to 2012. [Kim Jong Un](/wiki/Kim_Jong_Un "Kim Jong Un") succeeded his father as leader of North Korea in 2011, while South Korea elected the first ever female president [Park Geun-hye](/wiki/Park_Geun-hye "Park Geun-hye") in the [2012 election](/wiki/2012_South_Korean_presidential_election "2012 South Korean presidential election").[[121]](#cite_note-124) The conservative Park Geun-hye administration was formally accused of corruption, bribery, and influence-peddling for the involvement of Park's close friend [Choi Soon-sil](/wiki/Choi_Soon-sil "Choi Soon-sil") in state affairs. There followed a series of [nationwide public demonstrations](/wiki/2016%E2%80%932017_South_Korean_protests "2016–2017 South Korean protests") from November 2016,[[122]](#cite_note-125) and she was removed from office.[[123]](#cite_note-126) After the fallout of Park's impeachment and dismissal, elections were held and [Moon Jae-in](/wiki/Moon_Jae-in "Moon Jae-in") of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(South_Korea,_2015) "Democratic Party (South Korea, 2015)") won the presidency, immediately taking office on May 10, 2017.[[124]](#cite_note-127) His tenure saw an improving political relationship with North Korea, some increasing divergence in the military alliance with the United States, and the successful hosting of [the Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics").[[125]](#cite_note-128) In April 2018, Park Geun-hye was sentenced to 24 years in jail and convicted of abuse of power and corruption.[[126]](#cite_note-129) [The COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_South_Korea "COVID-19 pandemic in South Korea") caused South Korea to record more deaths than births, resulting in a population decline for the first time on record.[[127]](#cite_note-130)

In March 2022, [Yoon Suk Yeol](/wiki/Yoon_Suk_Yeol "Yoon Suk Yeol"), the candidate of the conservative opposition [People Power Party](/wiki/People_Power_Party_(South_Korea) "People Power Party (South Korea)"), won a close [election](/wiki/2022_South_Korean_presidential_election "2022 South Korean presidential election") over Democratic Party candidate [Lee Jae-myung](/wiki/Lee_Jae-myung "Lee Jae-myung") by the narrowest margin in the history of the Sixth Republic and was sworn in on May 10, 2022.[[128]](#cite_note-131) He declared [martial law](/wiki/2024_South_Korean_martial_law "2024 South Korean martial law") on December 3, 2024, accusing the opposition of being pro-North Korean and conducting anti-state activities.[[129]](#cite_note-martial-132) After several hours, the National Assembly voted to nullify the declaration in a unanimous vote of 190/0, causing Yoon to end martial law early on December 4.[[130]](#cite_note-133) Yoon's actions resulted in [his impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Yoon_Suk_Yeol "Impeachment of Yoon Suk Yeol") on December 14, 2024, followed by his unanimous removal from office on April 4, 2025.[[131]](#cite_note-134)[[132]](#cite_note-135) Lee Jae-myung won the [election](/wiki/2025_South_Korean_presidential_election "2025 South Korean presidential election") to succeed Yoon, immediately taking office on June 4, 2025.[[133]](#cite_note-136)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of South Korea](/wiki/Geography_of_South_Korea "Geography of South Korea") and [Geology of South Korea](/wiki/Geology_of_South_Korea "Geology of South Korea")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Dinosaur_Ridge_of_Seoraksan.jpg/250px-Dinosaur_Ridge_of_Seoraksan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dinosaur_Ridge_of_Seoraksan.jpg)

Dinosaur Ridge of [Seoraksan](/wiki/Seoraksan "Seoraksan"), the highest mountain in the [Taebaek mountain range](/wiki/Taebaek_mountain_range "Taebaek mountain range") in the [Gangwon Province](/wiki/Gangwon_Province,_South_Korea "Gangwon Province, South Korea").

South Korea occupies the southern portion of the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), which extends some 1,100 km (680 mi) from the Continental and East Asian mainland. This mountainous peninsula is flanked by the [Yellow Sea](/wiki/Yellow_Sea "Yellow Sea") to the west and the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") to the east. Its southern tip lies on the [Korea Strait](/wiki/Korea_Strait "Korea Strait") and the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea"). The country, including all its islands, lies between latitudes [33°](/wiki/33rd_parallel_north "33rd parallel north") and [39°N](/wiki/39th_parallel_north "39th parallel north"), and longitudes [124°](/wiki/124th_meridian_east "124th meridian east") and [130°E](/wiki/130th_meridian_east "130th meridian east"). Its total area is 100,410 square kilometers (38,768.52 sq mi).[[5]](#cite_note-South_Korea_country_profile-6)
South Korea can be divided into four general regions: an eastern region of high mountain ranges and narrow coastal plains; a western region of broad coastal plains, [river basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"), and rolling hills; a southwestern region of mountains and valleys; and a southeastern region dominated by the broad basin of the [Nakdong River](/wiki/Nakdong_River "Nakdong River").[[134]](#cite_note-korea_geo-137) South Korea is home to three terrestrial ecoregions: [Central Korean deciduous forests](/wiki/Central_Korean_deciduous_forests "Central Korean deciduous forests"), [Manchurian mixed forests](/wiki/Manchurian_mixed_forests "Manchurian mixed forests"), and [Southern Korea evergreen forests](/wiki/Southern_Korea_evergreen_forests "Southern Korea evergreen forests").[[135]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-138) South Korea's terrain is mostly mountainous, most of which is not [arable](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"). [Lowlands](/wiki/Upland_and_lowland "Upland and lowland"), located primarily in the west and southeast, make up only 30% of the total land area. South Korea has [20 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_South_Korea "List of national parks of South Korea") and popular natural areas such as the [Boseong](/wiki/Boseong "Boseong") Tea Fields, [Suncheon Bay Ecological Park](/wiki/Suncheon_Bay_Ecological_Park "Suncheon Bay Ecological Park"), and [Jirisan](/wiki/Jirisan "Jirisan").[[136]](#cite_note-139)

About 3,000 islands lie off the western and southern coasts of South Korea, the vast majority small and uninhabited. [Jeju Island](/wiki/Jeju_Island "Jeju Island"), the country's largest island, is about 100 kilometers (62 miles) off the southern coast of South Korea. [Hallasan](/wiki/Hallasan "Hallasan"), a dormant [volcano](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"), is South Korea's highest point and reaches 1,950 meters (6,400 feet) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"). The easternmost islands of South Korea are [Ulleungdo](/wiki/Ulleungdo "Ulleungdo") and the [Liancourt Rocks](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks "Liancourt Rocks") (Dokdo/Takeshima), while [Marado](/wiki/Marado "Marado") and [Socotra Rock](/wiki/Socotra_Rock "Socotra Rock") are the southernmost islands of South Korea.[[134]](#cite_note-korea_geo-137)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of South Korea](/wiki/Climate_of_South_Korea "Climate of South Korea")

South Korea tends to have a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") and a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate"), and is affected by the [East Asian monsoon](/wiki/East_Asian_monsoon "East Asian monsoon"), with [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") heavier in summer during a short rainy season called *[jangma](/wiki/East_Asian_rainy_season "East Asian rainy season")*, which begins end of June and lasts through the end of July. In Seoul, the average January temperature range is −7 to 1 °C (19 to 34 °F), and the average August temperature range is 22 to 30 °C (72 to 86 °F). Winter temperatures are higher along the southern coast and considerably lower in the mountainous interior.[[138]](#cite_note-141) Summer can be uncomfortably hot and humid, with temperatures exceeding 30 °C (86 °F) in most parts of the country. South Korea has four distinct seasons; spring, summer, autumn and winter. Spring usually lasts from late March to early May, summer from mid-May to early September, autumn from mid-September to early November, and winter from mid-November to mid-March.

Rainfall is concentrated in the summer months of June through September. The southern coast is subject to late summer [typhoons](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") that bring strong winds, heavy rains and sometimes floods. The average annual precipitation varies from 1,370 millimeters (54 in) in Seoul to 1,470 millimeters (58 in) in Busan.

### Environment

Main articles: [Environment of South Korea](/wiki/Environment_of_South_Korea "Environment of South Korea"), [Pollution in South Korea](/wiki/Pollution_in_South_Korea "Pollution in South Korea"), and [Climate change in South Korea](/wiki/Climate_change_in_South_Korea "Climate change in South Korea")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/CheonggyecheonSeoul.jpg/250px-CheonggyecheonSeoul.jpg)](/wiki/File:CheonggyecheonSeoul.jpg)

[Cheonggyecheon](/wiki/Cheonggyecheon "Cheonggyecheon") river is a modern public recreation space in [downtown Seoul](/wiki/Downtown_Seoul "Downtown Seoul")

During the first 20 years of South Korea's growth surge, little effort was made to preserve the environment.[[139]](#cite_note-google.com-142) Unchecked industrialization and urban development have resulted in deforestation and destruction of wetlands such as the Songdo Tidal Flat.[[140]](#cite_note-143) However, there have been recent efforts to balance these problems, including a government run $84 billion five-year [green growth](/wiki/Green_growth "Green growth") project that aims to boost energy efficiency and green technology.[[141]](#cite_note-144)

The climate initiative utilizes nearly two percent of the national GDP and facilitates creation of a nationwide bike network, solar and wind energy, decreasing combustion vehicles, backing daylight saving time and replacing obsolete lighting products in favor of environmentally friendly technologies such as LEDs.[[142]](#cite_note-South_Korea's_green_new_deal-145) The country plans to build a nationwide next-generation network that will be 10 times faster than broadband facilities, in order to reduce energy usage.[[142]](#cite_note-South_Korea's_green_new_deal-145)

The [renewable portfolio standard](/wiki/Renewable_portfolio_standard "Renewable portfolio standard") program with [renewable energy certificates](/wiki/Renewable_Energy_Certificate_(United_States) "Renewable Energy Certificate (United States)") runs from 2012 to 2022.[[143]](#cite_note-146)
Quota systems favor large, vertically integrated generators and multinational electric utilities because certificates are generally denominated in units of one megawatt-hour. They are also more difficult to design and implement than a [feed-in tariff](/wiki/Feed-in_tariff "Feed-in tariff").[[144]](#cite_note-wind-works.org-147) Around 350 residential [micro combined heat and power](/wiki/Micro_combined_heat_and_power "Micro combined heat and power") units were installed in 2012.[[145]](#cite_note-148) In 2017, South Korea was the world's seventh largest emitter of carbon emissions and the fifth largest emitter per capita. President Moon Jae-in pledged to reduce [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") to zero in 2050.[[146]](#cite_note-149)[[147]](#cite_note-150)

Seoul's [tap water](/wiki/Tap_water "Tap water") recently became safe to drink, with city officials branding it "[Arisu](/wiki/Arisu "Arisu")" in a bid to convince the public.[[148]](#cite_note-151) Efforts have also been made with [afforestation](/wiki/Afforestation "Afforestation") projects; South Korea had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 6.02/10, ranking it 87th globally out of 172 countries.[[149]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-152) Another multibillion-dollar project was the restoration of [Cheonggyecheon](/wiki/Cheonggyecheon "Cheonggyecheon"), a stream running through [downtown Seoul](/wiki/Downtown_Seoul "Downtown Seoul") that had previously been paved over by a motorway.[[150]](#cite_note-153) One major challenge is air quality, with acid rain, sulfur oxides, and annual yellow dust storms; however, many of these difficulties are a result of South Korea's proximity to China, which is a major air polluter.[[139]](#cite_note-google.com-142)

South Korea is a member of the [Antarctic-Environmental Protocol](/wiki/Protocol_on_Environmental_Protection_to_the_Antarctic_Treaty "Protocol on Environmental Protection to the Antarctic Treaty"), [Antarctic Treaty](/wiki/Antarctic_Treaty_System "Antarctic Treaty System"), [Biodiversity Treaty](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity"), [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol") (forming the Environmental Integrity Group (EIG), regarding [UNFCCC](/wiki/United_Nations_Framework_Convention_on_Climate_Change "United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change"),[[151]](#cite_note-154) with Mexico and Switzerland), [Desertification](/wiki/United_Nations_Convention_to_Combat_Desertification "United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification"), [Endangered Species](/wiki/CITES "CITES"), [Environmental Modification](/wiki/Environmental_Modification_Convention "Environmental Modification Convention"), [Hazardous Wastes](/wiki/Basel_Convention "Basel Convention"), [Law of the Sea](/wiki/United_Nations_Convention_on_the_Law_of_the_Sea "United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea"), [Marine Dumping](/wiki/Convention_for_the_Prevention_of_Marine_Pollution_by_Dumping_from_Ships_and_Aircraft "Convention for the Prevention of Marine Pollution by Dumping from Ships and Aircraft"), [Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty](/wiki/Comprehensive_Nuclear-Test-Ban_Treaty "Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty") (not into force), [Ozone Layer Protection](/wiki/Montreal_Protocol "Montreal Protocol"), [Ship Pollution](/wiki/MARPOL_73/78 "MARPOL 73/78"), [Tropical Timber 83](/wiki/International_Tropical_Timber_Agreement,_1983 "International Tropical Timber Agreement, 1983"), [Tropical Timber 94](/wiki/International_Tropical_Timber_Agreement,_1994 "International Tropical Timber Agreement, 1994"), [Wetlands](/wiki/Ramsar_Convention "Ramsar Convention"), and [Whaling](/wiki/International_Convention_for_the_Regulation_of_Whaling "International Convention for the Regulation of Whaling").[[152]](#cite_note-CIA-155)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of South Korea](/wiki/Government_of_South_Korea "Government of South Korea") and [Politics of South Korea](/wiki/Politics_of_South_Korea "Politics of South Korea")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  |  |
| [Lee Jae Myung](/wiki/Lee_Jae_Myung "Lee Jae Myung") [President](/wiki/President_of_South_Korea "President of South Korea") | [Kim Min-seok](/wiki/Kim_Min-seok_(politician) "Kim Min-seok (politician)") [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_South_Korea "Prime Minister of South Korea") |

The South Korean government's structure is determined by the [Constitution of the Republic of Korea](/wiki/Constitution_of_South_Korea "Constitution of South Korea"). Like many democratic states,[[153]](#cite_note-156) South Korea has a government divided into three branches: [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), [judicial](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary"), and [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature"). The executive and legislative branches operate primarily at the national level, although various ministries in the executive branch also carry out local functions. The [judicial branch](/wiki/Judiciary_of_South_Korea "Judiciary of South Korea") operates at both the national and local levels. Local governments are semi-autonomous and contain executive and legislative bodies of their own. South Korea is a constitutional democracy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Yeouido2025.jpg/250px-Yeouido2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yeouido2025.jpg)

Panoramic view of [Yeouido](/wiki/Yeouido "Yeouido") and the [National Assembly building](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_South_Korea "National Assembly of South Korea") from [Dangsan Railway Bridge](/wiki/Dangsan_Railway_Bridge "Dangsan Railway Bridge").

The constitution has been revised several times since its first promulgation in 1948 at independence. However, it has retained many broad characteristics and with the exception of the short-lived [Second Republic of Korea](/wiki/Second_Republic_of_Korea "Second Republic of Korea"), the country has always had a presidential system with an independent chief executive.[[154]](#cite_note-157) Under its current constitution the state is sometimes referred to as the [Sixth Republic of Korea](/wiki/Sixth_Republic_of_South_Korea "Sixth Republic of South Korea"). The first direct [election](/wiki/Elections_in_South_Korea "Elections in South Korea") was also held in 1948. South Korean politics is broadly divided into the [liberal camp](/wiki/Liberalism_in_South_Korea "Liberalism in South Korea"), currently represented chiefly by the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(South_Korea,_2015) "Democratic Party (South Korea, 2015)"), and the [conservative camp](/wiki/Conservatism_in_South_Korea "Conservatism in South Korea"), represented by the [People Power Party](/wiki/People_Power_Party "People Power Party").[[155]](#cite_note-158)

Although South Korea experienced a series of military dictatorships from the 1960s until the 1980s, it has since developed into a successful [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy"). Today, the [CIA World Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook") describes South Korea's democracy as a "fully functioning modern democracy",[[156]](#cite_note-ciawfb-159) while *[The Economist Democracy Index](/wiki/The_Economist_Democracy_Index "The Economist Democracy Index")* classifies it as a "flawed democracy", ranking at 32th out of 167 countries in 2024.[[157]](#cite_note-index2024-160) According to the [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices") South Korea is the 46th most electoral democratic country in the world as of 2024.[[158]](#cite_note-l511-161) However, some political experts has argued that South Korea had experienced [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding") and the reemergence of [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism"), particularly under the presidency of [Yoon Suk Yeol](/wiki/Yoon_Suk_Yeol "Yoon Suk Yeol"), which culminated when he declared [martial law](/wiki/2024_South_Korean_martial_law "2024 South Korean martial law") for the first time since the [1980 military coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_of_May_Seventeenth "Coup d'état of May Seventeenth") after the [assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_Park_Chung_Hee "Assassination of Park Chung Hee") of dictator [Park Chung Hee](/wiki/Park_Chung_Hee "Park Chung Hee"), and the first since [democratization in 1987](/wiki/June_Democratic_Struggle "June Democratic Struggle").[[159]](#cite_note-162)[[160]](#cite_note-:1-163) South Korea is ranked 31st on the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") (7th in the [Asia–Pacific](/wiki/Asia%E2%80%93Pacific "Asia–Pacific") region), with a score of 63 out of 100.[[161]](#cite_note-ti_2022-164)
